metadata.title: Material Plan Change: Stop Before the Scope Shifts

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Jambo, Liam — in for Bear. This is Liam, in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Liam, in for Bear?
props.segment: Material Plan Change: Stop Before the

beats.narration_text: He approved the plan. Six steps, clearly written, nothing alarming. Midway through, the task encountered a missing file. The agent installed a parsing library to handle the format, read a folder he had not explicitly named, and continued. The output was correct. None of those adaptations were in the plan he approved. He found out when he read the completion report. The scope had changed. The change had already happened. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, material plan change instruction in task brief

props.command: If at any point you need to deviate from this plan — different tools, new data, higher risk level — stop and tell me before proceeding.

beats.narration_text: Agents adapt. A tool fails — the agent tries another. A file is missing — the agent uses what it can find. A format is unexpected — the agent installs what it needs. These adaptations are features of the ReAct architecture, not errors. The question is not whether adaptation happens. The question is whether the adaptation changes what you authorized. Within scope: fine. Outside scope: stop and ask.
beats.new_visual_element: Agents always adapt — tool fails / file missing / format unexpected → within vs. outside scope
beats.spark_line: Adaptation is normal. Scope change is not.

beats.narration_text: Three changes that feel small from inside: reading one extra file because it seemed relevant, sending output to a shared folder for convenience, installing a library because the original format could not be parsed. Each is a scope expansion. Each adds data touched, permissions extended, or dependencies introduced. Small from the agent's perspective. Not small from yours.
beats.new_visual_element: Small feels normal — extra file / shared folder / library install → each is a scope expansion
beats.spark_line: Small from inside. Not small from outside.

beats.narration_text: The material-plan-change rule has three triggers: different tool than approved, new data beyond what was named, higher risk level than the original plan. If any trigger fires, stop before proceeding — not after. Reporting a scope change after the action is an audit log. Asking before the action is supervision. Only before catches the error.
beats.new_visual_element: Three triggers — different tool / new data / higher risk → stop before / after = audit / before = supervision
beats.spark_line: Before is supervision. After is audit.

beats.narration_text: The material-plan-change rule is one instruction added to the task brief. It does not slow the agent when the plan holds. It surfaces the moment the plan breaks — which is the moment when supervision has value. An agent that reports scope changes after completing them has taken the action you did not authorize. An agent that stops and asks has returned control at the right moment.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — plan with material-change clause / stop-and-ask output / before vs. after comparison
beats.spark_line: One instruction. Returns control at the right moment.

beats.narration_text: Add this clause to every agentic task brief. Paste this.

props.command: Add this clause to any agentic task: 'If completing this task requires you to (1) use a tool not in the approved plan, (2) access data not explicitly named, or (3) take an action at a higher risk level than described — stop immediately and report what you would do and why, before proceeding. Do not continue until I respond.'

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Material Plan Change: Stop Before the Scope Shifts] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
